coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have a profound effect on elimination of bacteria, fungi, and viruses, but the lack of an exact mechanism to interact with bacterial cells limits their development. Herein, we separated the CDs derived from chlorhexidine gluconate into three groups with uniformly small-scale, middle-scale, and large-scale particle sizes by using different molecular weight cut-off membranes. These positively charged particles exhibit significant antibacterial activity against the Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli and the Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus; they can cause an increase in bacterial cell permeability, synergistic destabilization, and broken integrity of the plasma membrane. Impressively, we found that antibacterial activity increases as the size of the CDs decreases. This phenomenon may stem from the differences in cellular uptake and distribution of CDs in the plasma membrane or restriction between the polar functional group and DNA molecule. Our study of the size effect as a target may improve the understanding of killing microorganisms by antibacterial CD drugs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of separating different particle sizes through dialysis membranes with different cut-off molecular weights. (A1-A3) TEM images (scale bar is 10 nm), (B1-B3) HRTEM images of the highly crystalline region, (C1-C3) particle size distribution statistics, and (D1-D3) AFM images for s-CGCDs (1), m-CGCDs (2), and l-CGCDs (3).

(A) UV–visible absorption of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, (B) emission wavelength versus CD size, (C) FT-IR spectra, and (D) Raman spectra of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs. (Black, red, and green represent s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, respectively.) (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(A1-A3) C 1 s, (B1-B3) N 1 s, and (C1-C3) O 1 s spectra for s-CGCDs (1), m-CGCDs (2), and l-CGCDs (3), respectively.

(A, B) Inhibition zone photos of the control group, gentamicin (40 μg/mL), CG (400 μg/mL), s-CGCDs (400 μg/mL), m-CGCDs (400 μg/mL), and l-CGCDs (400 μg/mL) against E. coli and S. aureus (3 × 107 CFU/mL) on MHA medium for 24 h and schematic diagram of the inhibition zone area and (C, D) their quantitative data of inhibition diameter. Error bars show the mean ± SD; n = 3. Significance was assessed using Student’s t-test, giving p values, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

(A) Typical photographs of E. coli and S. aureus (1 × 108 CFU/mL) after treatment with different concentrations of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs on LB nutrient agar medium for 24 h; (B-C) and their corresponding bacterial survival rates. Error bars show the mean ± SD; n = 3. Significance was assessed using Student’s t-test, giving p values, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

(A-B) The O.D. 540 nm value of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs treated with E. coli and S. aureus (5 × 106 CFU/mL) in LB broth medium for 24 h. (C-D) Comparison of the MBCs and MICs of CGCDs with various antibacterial metal-based NPs (Ag NPs [54], Au NPs [55], ZnO QDs [56], TiO2 NPs [57], and MoS2 QDs [9]) and CDs without postmodification (carbon dots derived from precursor Vitamin C [18], Arginine [58], Glucose + Ethylenediamine [59], Genipin + Chlorhexidine acetate [60], Bnzene + PEG [61], and Adenosine triphosphate + Lanthanum [62]). Error bars show the mean ± SD; n = 3. Significance was assessed using Student’s t-test, giving p values, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

(A) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus untreated and treated with 75 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs in LB broth medium for 6 h (the illustration is the enlarged image of bacterial cells).

Fluorescent images of E. coli (75 μg/mL) and S. aureus (50 μg/mL) untreated and treated with s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs in LB broth medium. Cells were stained with Hoechst 33,342 and PI. Blue and red fluorescent stains represent living/necrotic cells and dead/compromised cells respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Schematic diagram of possible antibacterial differences of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs against bacterial cells. (B) Zeta potentials of E. coli and S. aureus treated with s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs for 30 min. (C) Gel electrophoretic images of plasmid DNA (from E. coli) treated with s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs. label 1: control (plasmid DNA only); label 2: l-CGCDs (20 μg/mL); label 3: m-CGCDs (20 μg/mL); label 4: s-CGCDs (20 μg/mL); label 5: s-CGCDs (30 μg/mL). Error bars show the mean ± SD; n = 3. Significance was assessed using Student’s t-test, giving p values, *P < 0.05, **P < 0.01.

Schematic representation of the strategy employed for the preparation of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs and their antibacterial effects. A novel type of positively charged CDs is designed to realize significant bacteriostatic effects and is separated into different particle sizes for comparison.

head.ce:title: Insight into the effect of particle size distribution differences on the antibacterial activity of carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have a profound effect on elimination of bacteria, fungi, and viruses, but the lack of an exact mechanism to interact with bacterial cells limits their development. Herein, we separated the CDs derived from chlorhexidine gluconate into three groups with uniformly small-scale, middle-scale, and large-scale particle sizes by using different molecular weight cut-off membranes. These positively charged particles exhibit significant antibacterial activity against the Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli and the Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus; they can cause an increase in bacterial cell permeability, synergistic destabilization, and broken integrity of the plasma membrane. Impressively, we found that antibacterial activity increases as the size of the CDs decreases. This phenomenon may stem from the differences in cellular uptake and distribution of CDs in the plasma membrane or restriction between the polar functional group and DNA molecule. Our study of the size effect as a target may improve the understanding of killing microorganisms by antibacterial CD drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: The application of nanoparticles (NPs) in biological imaging, diagnosis, drug delivery, and resistance therapy holds the potential to improve current strategies for in vivo bacterial infection treatments [1–3]. Metal-containing NPs can generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and release metal ions, such as the noble metal NP series (e.g., Ag NPs, Cu NPs, and Au NPs), the metal oxide series (e.g., ZnO, TiO2, and MoO3), and the composite nanocluster series (e.g., Cu-Te and Cu2WS4), thereby substantially increasing the antimicrobial efficiency [4–9]. However, ensuing changes in oxidative stress, enzymatic inhibition, protein inactivation, and DNA damage severely hamper the survival of normal cells and leads to serious damage to healthy tissues, which diametrically contributes to the limitation of clinical application and failure of treatment [10]. Several carbon-based nanomedicines are being developed to mitigate these risks. One of the advantages of carbon elements is their connection with each other through multiform approaches to form diverse carbon allotropes, generating a succession of carbon antibacterial nanomaterials (e.g., graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and carbon nanodots) [11]. Due to the specific physicochemical properties (e.g., size and surface charge) of carbon dots (denoted CDs) in the antibacterial process, they are expected to be promising potential drugs for drug resistance, biofilms, and intracellular active/latent bacteria.

Additionally, CDs are characterized by light-activated activity, excellent photoluminescence properties, diverse surface functions, microbial adhesion, good water solubility, and biocompatibility [12–14]. Apart from these, synthetic procedures are fairly simple, directly reacting in solvents (e.g., water and organic matter) and using small molecule compounds as precursors [12]. Therefore, CDs could be a new antibacterial agent for biomedical applications both in vitro and in vivo. Previously, in a therapy related to bacteria with specific oxygen requirements, Liu et al. reported that CDs synthesized by metronidazole had antibacterial activity against the obligate anaerobe Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis) [15]. Another study, by Otis and coworkers, found that CDs derived from aminoguanidine and citric acid could inhibit the growth of Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa) [16]. However, these studies lack the same properties as other strains, such as anaerobic and facultatively anaerobic strains. In current antibacterial therapy tactics of CDs, more than half of the single bacterial inhibition CDs have been replaced by multifunctional CDs. Ju et al. have recently given versatility to antibacterial and prepared excitation-dependent multicolor emissive CDs against the Gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) and the Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) via a solvothermal reaction of chloroform, diethylamine, and ammonium carbonate [17]. Similarly, Li et al. showed that CDs obtained from natural substances by electrochemical methods have the same function. Furthermore, many recent reviews have been written about the modification of CDs, which promote the antibacterial field of CDs [18]. The Wu group developed a series of quaternized CDs to selectively interact with Gram-positive bacteria, such as S. aureus, Micrococcus luteus (M. luteus), and Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), and ultimately lead to death. This technique is faster and more precise than the conventional Gram-staining method [19,20]. Kováčová et al. investigated photoactive hydrophobic CDs/polyurethane nanocomposites (efficiently generating singlet oxygen) against two different bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus), and the antibacterial effect was remarkably improved [21]. Nonetheless, to the best of our knowledge, numerous mechanisms of CDs in the antibacterial field have rarely been reported.

Various approaches have been explored to the photocatalytic reaction (i.e., ROS-mediated sterilization) for microorganism killing. With CDs, the mechanism of ROS generation is often clear, but unfortunately, intrinsic light-activated properties are rare [22–24]. To date, factors arising from surface functionalization and surface charge are known to influence the antibacterial effect. Our screen focused on some discoveries that the surface charge of CDs could influence antibacterial treatments. The most representative work was published by the Qu group in 2016, who prepared CDs with three different charges (called S-CDs, C-CDs, and G-CDs, respectively) for experiments [25]. The results show that E. coli and B. subtilis exhibited signs of programmed cell death after treatment with positively charged S-CDs or negatively charged C-CDs, such as fractured DNA, extracellular exposure of phosphatidylserine, and condensation of the chromosome. Interestingly, these phenomena will not occur with uncharged G-CDs. Furthermore, inspired by the antibacterial mechanism of quaternary ammonium salt, Jian et al. studied a novel supercationic CDs from spermidine (a biogenic polyamine) for bacterial keratitis as an eye drop, and reported that its zeta potential can reach + 45 mV [26]. The efficient antibacterial agent supercationic CDs inhibits conventional S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and Salmonella enterica serovar typhimurium (S. typhimurium), as well as multidrug-resistant bacteria, (e.g., methicillin-resistant S. aureus). As a result, we presumed that CDs with positive surface charges might have a greater killing effect on bacteria than CDs with negative charges and neutral charges in some situations [27]. In addition to the surface charge of CDs, various functional groups seem to be another regulatory factor of death in a bacterial cell. Travlou et al. demonstrated that N-doped CDs showed a much higher antibacterial activity than S-doped CDs, which can be explained by the difference in the surface chemistry of CDs [28]. Specifically, the advantages of nitrogen functional groups were attributed to the protonated forms of amines and amides, which could interact with the negatively charged bacterial cell membrane, corresponding to the research outlined above. Although sulfur-containing CDs can rely on sulfhydryl groups for antibacterial effects, they are relatively weak due to the negative charge of the dissociation of carboxylic and sulfonic groups and sulfates.

In this study, we expanded on our previous conclusions regarding the charge distribution and functional groups of CDs and sought to grasp the essence of these strategies. We used the same method of carbonizing chlorhexidine gluconate (CG) to synthesize chlorhexidine gluconate carbon dots (CGCDs) [29] and then divided them into three different particle sizes to investigate the changes in antibacterial activity; namely, ~2.0 nm small particle size CGCDs (s-CGCDs), ~3.9 nm middle particle size CGCDs (m-CGCDs), and ~5.3 nm large particle size CGCDs (l-CGCDs) (Scheme 1 ). The three groups of CGCDs have similar properties because of the same precursor but show differences in photoluminescence (PL) performance and bacterial inhibition. Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus were chosen as model pathogenic bacteria that are frequently used in molecular biology [30]. Ultimately, we aimed to evaluate the differences in antibacterial activity due to the particle size varying from 2 to 10 nm through the inhibition zone, spread plate, broth microdilution methods, morphological analysis and live/dead staining of bacterial cells. Subsequently, we plan to carry out bacterial cell experiments to explore the specific mechanism and analyze the impairment of the permeability and integrity of the plasma membrane and damage to the DNA caused by different scale CGCDs.

We have tried to synthesize antibacterial CDs using various types of carbon sources as precursors, such as citric acid and urea, folic acid, glucose, and orange juice. Unfortunately, the experimental results showed that none of these CDs have the ability to kill bacterial cells. Eventually, we chose the biguanide antimicrobial agent to prepare CDs. According to a previous report, CGCDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method using a Teflon-lined reactor. Five milliliters of CG solution (19%~21% aqueous solution) was dispersed in ~ 15 mL deionized water, which provided the C and N sources. After 6 h of heat at 180 °C, CGCDs were collected by filtering the resulting suspension with a 0.22 µm microporous aqua-system cellulose membrane to remove precipitation of excessive carbonization. Then the obtained pale-yellow suspension was dialyzed with a cellulose fiber membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 100–500 Da) for 4 days to remove unreacted precursors. Note that the continuous and frequent replacement of deionized water for a long time was required because the drugs clinging to the CGCDs may affect the results of antibacterial tests. The dialysis solution was frozen and freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain a light yellow CGCD powder (0.060–0.086 g), which was stored in a dry atmosphere until use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of as-prepared CGCDs as s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain CGCDs with different particle size distributions, the as-prepared samples were separated by a series of molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) regenerated cellulose dialysis bags (separation membrane method) (upper part of Fig. 1 ) [31,32]. After the CGCD powder (1 g) was dissolved in a blend of 50% ethanol and 50% water, 8 kDa MWCO dialysis bags loaded with the mixture solution were placed in deionized water (100 mL) for 48 h, and < 7 nm CGCDs were excluded until the color was nearly transparent. The external dialysis fluid was collected and concentrated using a rotary evaporator. The concentrated solution was then passed through a 3.5 kDa MWCO membrane to remove < 5 nm CGCDs, and l-CGCDs (5.0 ~ 7.0 nm) were obtained from the interior. The procedure was repeated using the remaining exterior liquid. The last solute was divided into m-CGCDs (3.0 ~ 4.5 nm) and s-CGCDs (1.0 ~ 2.5 nm) with a 1 kDa MWCO dialysis bag.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by a UH5300 (Hitach, Japan) double beam UV–Vis-NIR spectrometer. The PL spectra were monitored with a FluoroSENS (Gilden Photonics, UK) fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a xenon lamp excitation source. Functional group information of the sample surfaces was recorded with a NicoletTM iSTM 50 (Thermo Fisher, USA) Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to analyze the crystallinity of CGCDs with a D8 ADVANCE (Bruker, Germany) X-ray diffractometer. The full spectrum and high-resolution spectra were collected through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), accomplished by an EscaLab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Raman spectra were obtained for s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs by using a Labram HR 800 (HORIBA Jobin Yvon, French) spectrometer at 514 nm. To avoid the fluorescence interference of CDs, other parameters need to be fine-tuned.

The morphologies of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs were observed by using a JEM-2100F (JEOL, Japan) high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) operated at 200 kV. The height distribution of the resulting s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs was determined using a Dimension Icon (Bruker, Germany) atomic force microscope (AFM). Surface charges and hydration particle size were determined by zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) tests with a Nano-ZS 90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) nanosizer.

Gram-negative E. coli (ATCC 25922) and Gram-positive S. aureus (ATCC 25923) were purchased from Nanjing Lezhen Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The strains were used for antibacterial experiments in the following experiments and stored at −80 °C. The strains were cultured in Luria-Bertani (LB) broth medium at 37 °C. The concentration of bacterial cells was determined by measuring the absorbance of cell suspensions at 540 nm with a Synergy 2 microplate reader (BioTek, USA). The bacterial suspensions were regularized to the desired concentrations with LB broth medium before being used.

The inhibition zones of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs were tested against Gram-negative bacteria such as E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus via the cup-plate method according to Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) guidelines [33]. First, an aliquot of bacterial stock suspension was cultured overnight at 37 °C in LB broth and regularized to 3 × 107 CFU/mL. Then 100 μL of bacterial cell suspension was spread on Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) medium, and the Oxford Cup (6 mm inside of diameter) was placed on the surface of agar plates. The diameters of the inhibition zones were determined after 24 h of incubation at 37 °C. Subsequently, 400 μg/mL s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs and 40 μg/mL gentamicin were dissolved in Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB) medium and added to a sterile Oxford Cup (200 μL). After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the diameter of the inhibition zone around each sample was recorded as the antibacterial effect of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs.

The antibacterial activities of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs against E. coli and S. aureus were further determined by the spread plate method. Briefly, the bacterial cells (1 × 108 CFU/mL) were incubated with samples of a series of dilutions (5, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 150 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 12 h, followed by 30 μL of diluted (1000-fold) bacterial suspensions spread onto the LB nutrient agar media and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. In addition, a blank group was included in the experiment that did not contain the nanomaterial. Finally, the colony-forming units (CFUs) on the plates were counted to compare the antibacterial activity of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs.

For the broth microdilution measurement, 100 µL of each bacterial suspension (5 × 106 CFU/mL) in LB broth medium was added to the wells of a sterile 96-well microtiter plate already containing 100 µL of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs (5, 25, 50, 75, 100, and 150 μg/mL). The final volume in each well was 200 µL. Control wells were prepared with culture medium, bacterial suspension, and CGCDs. Specifically, negative control wells were treated with a bacterial suspension in LB broth medium, and blank control wells were treated with the same concentration of CGCDs in medium to eliminate background interference. The suspension of each well was mixed on a microplate shaker at 800 rpm for 1 min prior to incubation for 24 h under the cultivation conditions described above. Finally, the absorbance value of the mixture was determined by the Synergy 2 BioTek microplate reader at 540 nm. The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs) were the lowest concentration that inhibited bacterial cell growth after 24 h, and the minimum bactericidal concentrations (MBCs) were the lowest concentration that killed 100% of the bacterial cells.

To determine the antibacterial efficacy of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs and the morphological changes of bacterial cells, SEM studies were carried out based on a previous report [34]. Logarithmic growth phase cells of two tested bacterial strains (5 × 106 CFU/mL) were treated with all CGCDs at concentrations of 75 μg/mL (E. coli) and 50 μg/mL (S. aureus). The control group was incubated in LB broth at 37 °C for 6 h. After incubation, bacterial cells were collected by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 5 min) and washed three times with 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.2–7.4) and fixed with 2.5% (v/v) glutaraldehyde in PBS for 6 h at 4 °C. Moreover, the cells were further dehydrated gradually with a series of ethanol solutions (25%, 50%, 75%, 90%, and 100%). The samples were fixed on an SEM support and dried under vacuum. The obtained bacterial cells were sputter-coated with gold before being observed by SEM (JSM-5610 LV, JEOL) at an accelerating voltage of 15.0 kV.

A standard bacterial cell live/dead staining assay was performed using Hoechst 33,342 and PI to examine the plasma membrane permeability after being cultured together with the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs. The blue-colored Hoechst 33,342 dye can enter both intact and membrane-compromised bacterial cells, whereas the red-colored propidium iodide (PI) dye can only enter cells with damaged membranes. First, the bacterial cells were treated with samples (for E. coli: 75 μg/mL and for S. aureus: 50 μg/mL) for 2 h and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 3 min to remove LB broth medium. Then, the bacteria were dyed with PI (2.35 μM) and Hoechst 33,342 (0.39 μM) in the dark for 30 min and washed with PBS 3 times (5000 rpm, 3 min). Finally, the stained bacterial suspensions were examined using a Nikon Ti-E-A1R fluorescence microscope, and images of 10 randomly chosen fields of view were captured.

See the Supporting Information of the Materials and Methods section for details on the materials and reagents, the particle size statistics, quantum yields (QY), 13C NMR and 1H NMR spectra, embryotoxicity tests, cell viability studies, intracellular generation assessment of ROS, bacterial membrane permeability evaluation, the relative membrane potential of bacterial cells, and DNA agarose gel electrophoresis measurements of samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The size effect of NPs has shown unusual behavior in various aspects such as catalytic activity, optics, cell viability, protein folding and aggregation, because of the large surface area, wide band gap, and edge sites [35–38]. However, this size effect on antibacterial activity of CDs has hardly been investigated. Therefore, to use size distribution as the key step in the understanding of interactions with microbes, other variables must be kept consistent. To ensure the broad applicability and accuracy of this approach, it is critical to choose appropriate nanocarriers and efficient separation methods. Thus, we chose CDs for our experiments, owing to their quantum effects and surface-rich functional groups [12]. We used a separation membrane step, as the positive correlation between molecular weight and volume is confirmed under nearly the same substance density [39]. Characterization with TEM and AFM revealed the morphology, diameter, and height of a series of materials. As illustrated in Figure 1 A1-A3, the as-prepared s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs show almost spherical shapes and possess good dispersion. The average diameter was measured on some particles randomly selected from the pictures. The results show that the sizes are in the range of 1–10 nm with an average size of approximately 2.0 ± 0.3, 3.9 ± 0.6, and 5.3 ± 0.5 nm, respectively (Figure 1 C1-C3), which is in line with the trend in hydrodynamic size (Figure S1). As anticipated, the AFM photographs further demonstrate that the average height is ~ 0.6, ~1.7, and ~ 3.8 nm (Figure 1 D1-D3), indicating that the gradient of particle size had been established. A legible crystal lattice in the HRTEM image (Figure 1 B1-B3) provides evidence for the crystalline structure of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs (0.19, 0.21, and 0.22 nm, respectively), as did fast Fourier transform imaging (Figure S2 A1-A3) and line-profile analysis (Figure S2 B1-B3). These similar lattice spacings fit neatly into the graphene (100) planes with a d-spacing of 0.21 nm. Combined with the graphene (002) planes corresponding to XRD data, both planes show that the sp2 carbon crystal domain for the resulting materials was identical to previous reports (Figure S3) [40]. Taken together, the CGCDs are successfully partitioned into the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, and the small-size CGCDs are closer to the molecular drugs in spatial volume.

We hypothesized that the particle size diversity of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs will result in a change in optical performance (i.e., the PL phenomenon). To test this possibility, we first used the UV–Vis absorption spectra as the detection means, which was chosen to determine the electron transition properties varied in the energy band, as illustrated in Fig. 2 A. The peak at ~ 221 nm was assigned to the characteristic π − π* transition of aromatic CC sp2 domains related to the carbon core, which cannot be observed in the fluorescence signal [41]. On the other hand, the peak at ~ 250 nm belongs to a change in the π − π* energy gap from CN bonds in the carbon core. Furthermore, a typical well-resolved nO/N − π* transition of electrons at ~ 310 nm is shown by s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs [42]. In accordance with the increased dimensions, the peak of the π − π* transition is redshifted by a few wavelengths. Prior to the conclusion, we resorted to being more thorough about the changes in the orbitals or states in the electronic structures. Figure S4 shows the excitation wavelength-dependent PL spectra of the CGCDs with diverse sizes excited every 20 nm wavelength. The specimens exhibited maximum PL peaks at 436 nm (s-CGCDs), 468 nm (m-CGCDs), and 489 nm (l-CGCDs), implying blue-violet to blue-green within the region of the visible spectrum (Fig. 2B). With quinine sulfate as the standard, the fluorescent QY for the single fractions after purification in water were 2.6% for s-CGCDs, 11.3% for m-CGCDs, and 8.0% for l-CGCDs (Table S1) [43]. These lower QY values indicated that the PL is not relevant for brilliant fluorescent molecules formed during carbonization, which was in accordance with the literature [44]. The comparison of the UV–Vis absorption and PL emission spectra revealed that certain electronic transitions could not be readily observed in the graph, and the redshift of the emission wavelength may be caused by nitrogen form variance and the deviation for sp2-sp3 hybridized atomic domains [45,46].

To quantify the chemical composition and functional group ratio of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, we captured the tiny disparity of structural information in different particle sizes. We evaluated the components of CGCDs, and XPS analysis was employed to characterize the form of the surface group. Carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and chlorine were clearly seen (nitrogen content was near-identical in the three groups) (Figure S5). Moreover, the high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. 3. A1-A3 demonstrates several peaks at 284.4 (±0.2), 285.5 (±0.1), and 288.2 (±0.4) eV, representing the forms of (sp3-sp2) C C/CC, (sp3) C N/CO, and (sp2) (CO)-O, respectively (trivial C-Cl bonds were ignored) [47]. Coincidentally, in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3. C1-C3), the peaks of 531.5 (±0.3) and 533.1 (±0.3) eV show that oxygen atoms are present in the forms of (sp2) CO and (sp3) C O/O H, corresponding to the carbon atoms. A subsequent fine spectrum of N1s indicates three types of nitrogen bonds, 398.4 (±0.2), 399.8(±0.4), and 400.7 (±0.3) eV, and typify these into pyridinic N, (sp3) pyrrolic N, and (sp3) graphitic N (Fig. 3. B1-B3) [47]. A similar decline was observed for graphitic N at ~ 400.8 eV from s-CGCDs to l-CGCDs, which indicated that the redshifted emission, in this case, may be caused by the increase in graphitic N [48]. The quantitative analysis of carbon atom form is summarized in Table S2. In detail, (i) the content of CN/CO for the m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs (43.77% and 40.29%) illustrated from the integral area, as well as the constituent of (CO)-O (9.82% and 9.20%, respectively), which are roughly equal to s-CGCDs (44.70% and 11.01%, respectively); (ii) and for the content of CC/CC, both the proportions of m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs are more than s-CGCDs owing to the larger core of the particle size [49]. Fig. 2C is the FTIR spectrum of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, from which stretching vibrations of OH/NH (3450–3100 cm−1), –CH3/–CH2- (2950–2900 cm−1), CO/CN (1560–1530 and 1630–1580 cm−1), CC (1500–1480 cm−1), CN (1420–1350 cm−1), and OCO (1130–1060 cm−1) can be observed, which makes it possible to play a role in antibacterial function, in full accordance with the XPS data [19,50]. Raman spectroscopy showed a decreasing intensity of the d-band at 1358 cm−1 and an increasing intensity of the G-band at 1578 cm−1 from s-CGCDs to l-CGCDs (Fig. 2D). This behavior means there are a number of structurally embedded graphitic nitrogen atoms to the sp2 scaffold, which could be a consequence of a slightly higher number of defects in the l-CGCDs [51]. The broader G-band for s-CGCD can be explained by increasing disorder. Although ID/IG does not significantly reduce, it is enough to complete the transition of CGCDs from disorder into order. [47]. As a result of abundant amino groups, the zeta potential of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs in deionized water (and in 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline and 0.9% NaCl solution) were measured as + 35.9 (+7.99 and + 13.23) mV, +34.2 (+6.63 and + 12.09) mV, and + 32.3 (+6.61 and + 12.18) mV, which are higher than 0.5% wt.% CG aqueous solution (+9.77 mV) (Figure S6 and Table S3) [25]. We suspect that the surface charge of the CGCDs is strongly connected to antibacterial activity. Overall, these results show that some key components in various particle size distributions are a cause of differences in a few minor variations of PL. However, the nitrogen content, functional group type, and surface charge are completely similar and thus we can perform a comparison of antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of antibacterial activity of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs
ce:section.ce:para: We used Gram-negative E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus to evaluate the antibacterial activity of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs. Prior to the comparison, it was first confirmed that the CGCDs, prepared from precursors, had a significant inhibitory effect on microorganisms instead of the CG itself. 13C NMR showed that no characteristic peaks of CG were observed on CGCDs (D2O, δ 62.5, 70.8, 72.5, 74.0, and 178.5 ppm), suggesting that the drug molecules did not adhere to the surface after dialysis (Figure S7). To qualitatively compare the microbial abilities of the three particle size CGCDs, the diffusion disk method is a standard method recognized by the Clinical and Laboratory Standards Institute (CLSI) [33]. In this test, the diameter of the inhibition zone illustrated the sensitivity of the bacteria to materials; the better the drug's ability to diffuse and inhibit, the larger the diameter of the zone of inhibition. In addition to satisfactory antibacterial capability, the higher diffusion, dispersion, and water-soluble capacity of CDs are also imperative to promote the formation of the zone of inhibition. Fig. 4 , part A and part B, shows the photograph and inhibition zone diameter as a clear area around each white antibacterial disk. For example, the inhibition diameters of E. coli in response to s-CGCDs were approximately 25.88 mm, which is clearly larger than the zone diameters of ~ 19.15 and ~ 15.85 mm for m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs (Fig. 4C). For S. aureus, the inhibition diameters of s-CGCDs were ~ 30.68 mm in contrast to all CGCD drugs, which indicated that it possesses the highest antibacterial activity (Fig. 4D). The zone diameters of E. coli and S. aureus treated with the s-CGCDs (400 μg/mL) were compared with 40 μg/mL of gentamicin (an aminoglycoside antibiotic) and 400 μg/mL of CG solution. Interestingly, we found that the CGCDs exhibit similar inhibition zones to gentamicin, while the concentration difference is 10-fold, whereas they exhibit moderate gaps to CGs at the same concentration. These findings preliminarily suggest that the particle size has a certain influence on the antibacterial activity against both E. coli and S. aureus.

Encouraged by the excellent phenomenon of inhibition zone diameters, we then used the more accurate spread plate method [52]. The sensitivity of bacteria to high concentrations of drugs prevents them from growing on LB nutrient agar medium, and ultimately, the relative number of viable bacteria in the sample was estimated by counting the number of colonies on different samples and plates at different concentrations. Fig. 5 A and B present the antibacterial activity of all materials against the two strains via agar plates, and the growth status is exhibited in Table S4. The colony number results showed that s-CGCDs with concentrations above 100 μg/mL and m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs with concentrations above 150 μg/mL exerted strong antibacterial activity against the E. coli strain. Notably, Fig. 5 B and C show the corresponding statistics of the antibacterial effect, demonstrating that the number of colonies were significantly lower against the increase of concentrations incubated with s-CGCDs, compared with other samples. In terms of S. aureus antibacterial effect, the bacterial survival rate was observed with 50, 75, and 100 μg/mL of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, respectively, treated with the bacterial strains, converging towards zero. Similarly, small particle size s-CGCDs, as expected, play a significant role in bacterial inhibition, and the antibacterial effect decreased as the particle size increased. However, whether E. coli or S. aureus, this phenomenon basically weakened after the diameter of the particles reached ~ 6 nm (Figure S8). In contrast with the s-CGCDs and m-CGCDs, the antibacterial activity of huge particles (i.e., greater than 6 nm) against both E. coli and S. aureus remained at the same level under a series of concentrations (Figure S9). Moreover, the antibacterial stability of CGCDs against bacterial cells showed the same level after being placed for ten days (Figure S10). Impressively, the CGCDs show prominent inhibition efficiency on S. aureus compared to E. coli under the same conditions.

Owing to the commendable antibacterial activity of s-CGCDs demonstrated by the diffusion disk method and spread plate method, we explored the minimum inhibitory concentration (MICs) and minimal bactericidal concentration (MBCs) using a BioTek Synergy 2 microplate reader at an optical density of 540 nm [53]. The values of O.D. 540 showed that s-CGCDs were always lower than the other particle sizes within 0–150 μg/mL, especially remarkable at concentrations greater than 25 μg/mL (Fig. 6 A and B). In contrast, a decreasing trend of bacterial death was observed in both the m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs, which was in accordance with the results of the spread plate method. Accordingly, we summarized the MICs (Table S5, E. coli, s-CGCDs vs. m-CGCDs vs. l-CGCDs, 75 vs. 100 vs. 100 μg/mL; and S. aureus, 50 vs. 75 vs. 100 μg/mL) and MBCs (E. coli, s-CGCDs vs. m-CGCDs vs. l-CGCDs, 100 vs. 150 vs. 150 μg/mL; and S. aureus, 50 vs. 75 vs. 150 μg/mL) treated with s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, indicating effective inhibition of microorganism initiation by small-scale size. As a new class of carbon-based antibacterial nanomaterials, the MBCs and MICs of CGCDs were further evaluated by comparison with previously reported antibacterial metal-based NPs and CDs (Fig. 6 C and D). Compared to the others, CGCDs with different sizes exhibit bacterial inhibition capabilities close to those of metal-based NPs, and their inhibition effects are better than those reported for CDs for E. coli and S. aureus, which demonstrate their good antibacterial activity.

The current skin disinfection of a CG solution has been connected to side effects such as allergy symptoms, poor salt ion solution applicability, and many types of cytotoxicity (e.g., human periodontal ligament cells, human dermal fibroblasts, human osteoblastic cell line) [63]. Thus, alternative treatments that mitigate adverse reactions in vivo are warranted. Considering the potential risk of CGCDs in organisms, we evaluated the toxicological safety of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs [64]. No obvious mortality or abnormality rates were found in comparison with the negative control group according to the embryotoxicity analysis of sea urchins (Strongylocentrotus nudus) (Figure S11). Furthermore, L929 mouse fibroblast cells, a representative wound healing cell line, were also employed for cytotoxicity analysis and biocompatibility. s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs did not alter cell viability when compared with the CG group, which suggested no adverse effect on healthy tissue, such as protein oxidation caused by ROS (Figure S12). Therefore, the low toxicity, good stability, and excellent PL elicited by CGCDs in addition to their significant efficacy in inhibiting bacterial growth and propagation highlight the potential advantage of this treatment over biological applications.

Antibacterial studies of CDs are increasingly performed by using homologous series of samples in which only one attribute is varied at a time (e.g., size, morphology, functionality, and surface property). To ensure that other factors are approximately the same, we used the identical carbon precursor to eliminate the effect and fully explored the effect of particle size on it. CG (a cationic surface-active preservative) aqueous solution possesses a positive charge (Table S3) and shows a broad-spectrum antibacterial effect against negatively charged bacteria. The mechanism involved the leakage of cellular components by destroying the permeation barrier on the bacterial cytoplasmic membrane [65]. Interestingly, the CGCDs inherit the high positive charge characteristics of the precursor and are also stable in the physiological environment. Therefore, inspired by the antimicrobial principle of CG, we intend to further explore the antibacterial mechanism and differences of CGCDs. Through the introduction of 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), the intracellular ROS that engender oxidative stress under light or H2O2 can be investigated [66]. Considering the slight PL interference of CGCDs, we eliminated the effect of the materials at excitation of 485 nm and emission of 528 nm via a control group. As shown in Figure S13 for the kinetics study, the fluorescence intensity of the groups treated with s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs actually level off with one another. These results confirmed that intracellular ROS were not produced during the antibacterial process. We next investigated the structural integrity of the bacterial cell membrane under the three CGCD treatments. As shown in Fig. 7 , untreated control bacteria showed that the integrity of the plasma membrane of E. coli and S. aureus could be clearly observed. After treatment with s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, the plasma membranes had lost integrity and cytoplasmic components had leaked to their surface, especially in the s-CGCD groups. These results suggest that CGCDs lead to damage in the plasma membrane of E. coli and S. aureus, which is similar to the conditions caused by cationic antibacterial agents [67].

Compared with E. coli treated with CGCDs, S. aureus had a lower MIC value. In fact, the outermost layer of Gram-positive bacteria cells has a unique thick layer of peptidoglycan linked with negatively charged teichoic acid [20,72,73]. Notably, Pasquina-Lemonche et al. recently released the architecture of the cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria (e.g., B. subtilis and S. aureus), revealing that the disordered gel of the peptidoglycan (the primary component of the bacterial cell wall) layer consists of a landscape of large (up to 60 nm in diameter) and deep (up to 23 nm) pores on its surface, whereas the inner surface is much denser and glycan strand spacing is usually < 7 nm [74]. These findings suggest that Gram-positive bacterial cell walls can provide many anionic sites compared to the outer layer of the lipopolysaccharide of Gram-negative bacteria to facilitate the electrostatic binding of CGCDs [75]. Clathrin-mediated endocytosis is a fundamental method used by cells to internalize macromolecules and NPs [76]. However, ultrasmall NPs and molecules may simply diffuse through the lipid bilayer [39]. It is important to note that NPs embedded into the cells through different pathways are distributed differently in the membrane structure due to the size effect [77]. For example, studies by other groups demonstrated that the tannic acid-stabilized Au NPs distributed in lipid bilayers drastically increase when the size is decreasing (100 nm to 5 nm) [78]. Moreover, previous studies reported that 1–2 nm Au NPs have less effect on the lipid bilayer structure and possess higher HeLa cell toxicity than larger Au NPs [79,80]. Taken together, the reasons behind the increased bacterial plasma membrane damage caused by s-CGCDs might be different between the cellular uptake and distribution of s-CGCDs (~2.0 nm), m-CGCDs (~3.9 nm), and l-CGCDs (~5.3 nm) in lipid bilayers, which led to the differential antibacterial outcomes (Fig. 9A).

Stable binding with nucleic acids in biological media is one of the important factors in determining the antibacterial activity of NPs [81]. To further verify the difference, we analyzed the binding capacities of the CGCDs with DNA molecules via agarose gel electrophoresis. The plasmid DNA of E. coli has three main topological states, including supercoiled (SC), open circular (OC), and linear (L) conformers, which represent undamaged molecules and disruption of a phosphodiester bond on one or two strands, respectively [82]. Therefore, single-strand and double-strand breaks in helix DNA could be identified according to the disparate mobility in the gel electrophoresis (the intensity of the different bands implies the relative concentration of DNA conformers). As shown in Fig. 9C, the proportion of SC-conformation decreases, while the l-conformation and OC-conformation treated with s-CGCDs increases, which indicates that the small CGCDs could loosen the DNA structure and unwind the double helix structure. Based on previous studies, the interaction between CDs and DNA molecules has been reported to lead to DNA secondary structural distortions by the terminal base pairs binding with oxygen-containing groups of CDs by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic forces [18,25,26,83]. We hypothesized that –OH, –NH2, and –CONH2 polar groups on the surface of CGCDs may have an attraction effect on DNA molecules under a specific spatial structure; however, the steric hindrance of the carbon core sharply limits the reactivity. To design and optimize a test that addresses this trade-off, we built a parallel isotope reaction experiment from earlier work on regioselectivity and stereospecific deuteration of NPs [84,85]. We determined the 1H NMR spectra of s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs to study the degree of deuterium incorporation through different regioselectivities of isotope labeling reactions. The normalized integral area within 2.5–6 ppm results shows that small particle size has a higher degree of deuteration (for the protons attached with hydroxyl, ether and carbonyl groups) and lower steric hindrance, which could allow functional groups to adequately contact the DNA molecule and distort the conformation (Figure S15) (Fig. 9A).

Our approach demonstrated that positively charged antibacterial CGCDs exert inhibitory effects on bacterial cells, which was attributed to the increased permeability, synergistic destabilization, and broken integrity of the plasma membrane. Our results also suggest that the small size s-CGCDs show the best antibacterial effects in comparison with m-CGCDs and l-CGCDs, which could be due to their difference in cellular uptake and distribution or interaction with DNA molecules. Despite the multiple phenomena found, we do not fully understand the mechanism for the antibacterial activity of the s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs, which need further study. Moreover, as mentioned above, the high biological safety of CGCDs in toxicity evaluation might be attributed to the different structures outside the cell membrane, autophagy, and lysosome degradation in eukaryotic cells [86,87]. The barrier of the cell nucleus is also a key to resisting invasion, which could significantly reduce the disturbances or effects of external cellular processes, such as DNA replication and repair and RNA synthesis [88].

In summary, a mixture of full-size fluorescent CDs was prepared by the simple solvothermal of CG in water. The individual particle size fluorescent components were separated by membranes with different MWCO. We obtained s-CGCDs, m-CGCDs, and l-CGCDs with various particle size distributions (from ~ 2.0 nm to ~ 5.3 nm) and similar chemical compositions and elucidated the effect of their particle size on optical performance and antibacterial activity. The size-dependent PL change (i.e., the redshift) is highly attributed to the increased amount of graphitic nitrogen in the CD structure. Remarkably, these positively charged CGCDs (zeta potential ca. + 34 mV) destroy the permeability and integrity of the bacterial plasma membrane more effectively than conventional CDs [59,60,62], leading to marked inhibition of the Gram-negative bacteria E. coli and Gram-positive S. aureus growth. A subsequent systematic evaluation of CGCD-induced toxicity was performed, indicating that CGCDs can selectively kill bacteria while decreasing injury to the human body. Importantly, the smaller s-CGCDs in these particles have a significantly enhanced antibacterial activity compared to the other sizes, which might be due to the difference in cellular uptake and distribution in the plasma membrane. Furthermore, we found that the small size s-CGCDs could cause plasmid DNA damage, which may result from the binding of the surface polar functional group of the diminutive carbon core. Although conferring antibacterial activity to CDs is an area of active research, much of this work focuses less on the aspect of non-ROS mechanisms [21,23,24,89,90]. Our work revealed the mechanism of action of cationic antimicrobial CGCDs and reported its first experimental example of the size effect. Pointing out this size rule will help to unlock a deeper understanding of antibacterial activity-structure relationships, allowing researchers to better comprehend antibacterial CDs suited for killing microorganisms and a wide range of disinfectant applications. Future work should also consider the accurate regulation and control of surface functional groups and element content of CDs to thoroughly explore regions of antibacterial activity.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Xiaohong Chu: Formal analysis. Zhixuan Wang: Methodology. Xinrong Huang: Methodology. Jie Li: Methodology. Cheng Yao: Resources. Ninglin Zhou: Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Project administration. Jian Shen: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
